narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—applying to law school is a huge step, and it's heartbreaking to hear that our prediction model sees a 43.9% chance of you not passing the bar exam after graduation. Picture this as a story the model pieced together from your profile, starting with your Year 3 grades landing you in the bottom decile at school—that was the biggest red flag, signaling to the model a steep challenge in keeping up with the demands of legal studies. Your LSAT score of 28.3 built on that worry, as it fell short of what the model typically sees in students who power through to bar success. Even your solid Year 1 decile of 2 couldn't offset those patterns, pulling the story further toward struggle. Your upper-middle family income, while a strength in many ways, surprisingly nudged the outlook downward in this mix, perhaps because the model links it to certain competitive pressures. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 added another layer of concern, suggesting the academic rigor of law school might feel like an uphill climb. As a white female studying full-time, those details rounded out a profile that, in combination, painted a picture of higher risk—subtly influencing the model's view without dominating it. It's not that any one thing dooms you, but together they tell the model a cautious tale of potential hurdles ahead. The good news? This is just one data-driven snapshot, and with targeted steps—like boosting study strategies or retaking the LSAT—you could rewrite your own story. You're clearly driven to pursue this path, and that passion counts for a lot. Hang in there; law school dreams don't end here. With encouragement, [Your Name/ Admissions Team]
